note: Curated high-value subset — 98 techniques across all 14 tactics for SPECTRA workflows

techniques.name: Active Scanning
techniques.description: Adversaries may execute active reconnaissance scans to gather information that can be used during targeting. Active scans involve probing victim infrastructure directly.
techniques.detection: Monitor for suspicious network traffic that could indicate scanning activity such as large volumes of requests to varying ports or unusual protocol usage.

techniques.name: Gather Victim Identity Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's identity that can be used during targeting, including credentials, email addresses, and employee names.
techniques.detection: Monitor for suspicious attempts to gather employee information through social engineering or data breaches exposed on dark web forums.

techniques.name: Gather Victim Network Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's networks that can be used during targeting, including domain properties, DNS, network topology, and IP addresses.
techniques.detection: Monitor for indicators of network reconnaissance such as DNS enumeration, WHOIS lookups, and BGP route analysis targeting organizational assets.

techniques.name: Gather Victim Org Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's organization, including business relationships, physical locations, and organizational structure.
techniques.detection: Monitor for scraping of organizational websites, job postings, social media profiles, and public filings that may reveal internal structure or technology stacks.

techniques.name: Phishing for Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to elicit sensitive information that can be used during targeting, rather than to deliver malware or gain direct access.
techniques.detection: Monitor for inbound emails with social engineering indicators requesting sensitive information, credential harvesting links, or replies containing internal data.

techniques.name: Acquire Infrastructure
techniques.description: Adversaries may buy, lease, or rent infrastructure that can be used during targeting. This includes domains, DNS servers, VPS, and web services.
techniques.detection: Monitor for newly registered domains or infrastructure that may be associated with known adversary patterns or typosquatting of organizational assets.

techniques.name: Obtain Capabilities
techniques.description: Adversaries may buy, steal, or download software tools and exploits that can be used during targeting, including malware, exploits, certificates, and vulnerabilities.
techniques.detection: Monitor dark web marketplaces and exploit repositories for capabilities being acquired that target organizational technology stacks.

techniques.name: Compromise Accounts
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise accounts with services that can be used during targeting, such as social media, email, or cloud accounts to support operations.
techniques.detection: Monitor for compromised accounts being used in phishing campaigns, social engineering operations, or as infrastructure for adversary communications.

techniques.name: Develop Capabilities
techniques.description: Adversaries may build capabilities that can be used during targeting, including malware, exploits, self-signed certificates, and code-signing certificates.
techniques.detection: Monitor for new malware samples, custom exploit code, and newly generated certificates that may be associated with adversary development activities.

techniques.name: Phishing
techniques.description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems. This includes spearphishing attachments, links, via service, and spearphishing voice.
techniques.detection: Monitor for suspicious email attachments, links to credential harvesting sites, and unusual sender patterns or domain spoofing.

techniques.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to exploit vulnerabilities in internet-facing applications to gain initial access. Common targets include web servers, VPNs, and firewalls.
techniques.detection: Monitor application logs for abnormal input patterns, unexpected error codes, and known exploit signatures targeting web applications and network devices.

techniques.name: External Remote Services
techniques.description: Adversaries may leverage external-facing remote services to initially access a network. Remote services such as VPNs, Citrix, and other access mechanisms allow users to connect from external locations.
techniques.detection: Monitor for logon attempts to external remote services from unusual geographic locations, at unusual times, or using atypical client configurations.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts
techniques.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts to gain initial access, persistence, privilege escalation, or defense evasion.
techniques.detection: Monitor for logon events from unusual locations or devices, impossible travel scenarios, and usage of dormant or service accounts for interactive logons.

techniques.name: Supply Chain Compromise
techniques.description: Adversaries may manipulate products or product delivery mechanisms prior to receipt by a final consumer to compromise data or systems.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unexpected changes to software packages, verify hashes of downloaded software against known-good values, and track supply chain integrity.

techniques.name: Trusted Relationship
techniques.description: Adversaries may breach or leverage organizations who have access to intended victims through trusted third-party relationships such as IT service providers or contractors.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unusual activity from third-party provider accounts, connections from partner networks at odd hours, and escalation of third-party privileges.

techniques.name: Drive-by Compromise
techniques.description: Adversaries may gain access to a system through a user visiting a compromised website that exploits the user's browser or browser plugins.
techniques.detection: Monitor for browser exploit indicators including unexpected child processes from browsers, abnormal plugin behavior, and connections to known watering hole domains.

techniques.name: Replication Through Removable Media
techniques.description: Adversaries may move onto systems by copying malware to removable media and exploiting Autorun features when the media is inserted into a system.
techniques.detection: Monitor for file execution from removable media, new autorun configurations, and unexpected USB device connections in sensitive environments.

techniques.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries. Includes PowerShell, Windows Command Shell, Unix Shell, Python, VBScript, and JavaScript.
techniques.detection: Monitor for abnormal process creation events, unusual command-line arguments, and script execution with obfuscation or encoding techniques.

techniques.name: User Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may rely upon specific user actions to gain execution, such as opening a malicious file, clicking a link, or granting elevated permissions.
techniques.detection: Monitor for processes spawned from user-facing applications such as email clients, browsers, or document editors that execute suspicious child processes.

techniques.name: Windows Management Instrumentation
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute malicious commands and payloads on local and remote systems.
techniques.detection: Monitor for WMI process creation events (wmiprvse.exe spawning child processes), remote WMI connections, and suspicious WMI event subscriptions.

techniques.name: Scheduled Task/Job
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling to facilitate execution of malicious code. Includes Windows Task Scheduler, cron, at jobs, and systemd timers.
techniques.detection: Monitor for newly created scheduled tasks or cron jobs, especially those executing from unusual directories or with encoded/obfuscated command lines.

techniques.name: Inter-Process Communication
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse inter-process communication mechanisms to execute code. This includes Component Object Model (COM), Dynamic Data Exchange (DDE), and XPC Services.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unusual COM object creation, DDE protocol execution from Office applications, and unexpected inter-process communication patterns.

techniques.name: Native API
techniques.description: Adversaries may interact with the native OS application programming interface to execute behaviors, bypassing higher-level APIs and security monitoring.
techniques.detection: Monitor for direct syscall usage, calls to undocumented NT API functions, and processes making native API calls that bypass standard Windows API layers.

techniques.name: System Services
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse system services or daemons to execute commands or programs. Windows service execution and Launchctl are common methods.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for service creation events, execution via sc.exe or launchctl, and services configured to execute from unusual paths or with suspicious binary paths.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Client Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in client applications to execute code. Common targets include browsers, Office applications, and PDF readers.
techniques.detection: Monitor for client applications spawning unexpected child processes, abnormal memory operations, and processes exhibiting behavior inconsistent with normal application use.

techniques.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may configure system settings to automatically execute a program during boot or logon to maintain persistence. Registry Run Keys are a common method on Windows.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Creation
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor Registry Run/RunOnce key modifications, startup folder changes, and new entries in boot configuration that reference unknown executables.

techniques.name: Scheduled Task/Job
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling functionality to facilitate recurring execution of malicious code. Includes Windows Task Scheduler, cron, and at jobs.
techniques.detection: Monitor for newly created scheduled tasks or cron jobs, especially those executing from unusual directories or with encoded/obfuscated command lines.

techniques.name: Create or Modify System Process
techniques.description: Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence. Includes Windows services, systemd services, and Launch Daemons.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Creation
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for new service installations, modifications to existing service binaries or configurations, and creation of Launch Daemon or systemd unit files.

techniques.name: Event Triggered Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may establish persistence using system mechanisms that trigger execution based on specific events, such as WMI event subscriptions, AppInit DLLs, or accessibility features.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for WMI event subscriptions, changes to AppInit_DLLs registry keys, modification of accessibility feature binaries, and new shell profile entries.

techniques.name: Hijack Execution Flow
techniques.description: Adversaries may execute their own payloads by hijacking the way operating systems run programs, including DLL search order hijacking, DLL side-loading, and PATH interception.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for DLLs loaded from unusual locations, unexpected modifications to system PATH, and binaries placed in directories that precede legitimate search paths.

techniques.name: Create Account
techniques.description: Adversaries may create new accounts to maintain access to victim systems. This includes local accounts, domain accounts, and cloud accounts.
techniques.detection: Monitor for new account creation events, especially those created by non-administrative users, accounts with elevated privileges, or accounts created outside normal provisioning workflows.

techniques.name: Account Manipulation
techniques.description: Adversaries may manipulate accounts to maintain or elevate access, including adding credentials, modifying permissions, or adding accounts to privileged groups.
data_sources: Active Directory: Active Directory Object Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for modifications to account attributes, addition of accounts to privileged groups, SSH authorized_keys changes, and unexpected credential resets.

techniques.name: Server Software Component
techniques.description: Adversaries may abuse legitimate extensible components of servers to establish persistent access, including web shells, IIS modules, SQL stored procedures, and transport agents.
techniques.detection: Monitor for new or modified files in web server directories, unexpected IIS module registrations, new SQL stored procedures, and unusual server process behavior.

techniques.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
techniques.description: Adversaries may circumvent mechanisms designed to control elevate privileges to gain higher-level permissions. UAC bypass on Windows is a prevalent technique.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for processes requesting elevation outside of normal patterns, manipulation of UAC-related registry keys, and unexpected auto-elevation behavior.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
techniques.description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities to escalate privileges. These exploits target kernel, driver, or service vulnerabilities to gain SYSTEM or root access.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unusual process behavior indicative of exploitation such as unexpected privilege changes, process injection, or abnormal kernel driver loading.

techniques.name: Access Token Manipulation
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify access tokens to operate under a different user or system security context to perform actions and bypass access controls.
techniques.detection: Monitor for token manipulation API calls (AdjustTokenPrivileges, DuplicateToken), unexpected privilege escalation, and processes running under mismatched user contexts.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts
techniques.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining privilege escalation where those accounts have higher privileges.
techniques.detection: Monitor for use of privileged accounts from unusual sources, privilege escalation through group membership changes, and service account misuse.

techniques.name: Process Injection
techniques.description: Adversaries may inject code into processes to evade defenses and elevate privileges. Techniques include DLL injection, process hollowing, thread execution hijacking, and APC injection.
techniques.detection: Monitor for processes allocating memory in other processes, writing to remote process memory, creating remote threads, and loading unexpected DLLs.

techniques.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may configure system settings to automatically execute a program during boot or logon that runs with elevated privileges to escalate.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Creation
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for autostart entries that execute with SYSTEM or root privileges, modifications to services running as elevated accounts, and boot-time persistence with high privilege contexts.

techniques.name: Create or Modify System Process
techniques.description: Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes that run with elevated privileges, providing persistent privileged execution.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Creation
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for new services configured to run as SYSTEM or root, modifications to existing privileged service binaries, and unusual service account configurations.

techniques.name: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify the configuration settings of a domain or identity tenant to evade defenses and escalate privileges in centralized directory services.
data_sources: Active Directory: Active Directory Object Creation
Active Directory: Active Directory Object Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for modifications to Group Policy Objects, trust relationships between domains, and changes to Azure AD tenant policies that could weaken security controls.

techniques.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to make payloads and files difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating their contents.
techniques.detection: Monitor for files with high entropy, unusual encoding patterns, or known packer signatures, and processes that decode or decrypt content at runtime.

techniques.name: Impair Defenses
techniques.description: Adversaries may maliciously modify components of a victim environment to hinder or disable defensive mechanisms. This includes disabling security tools and logging.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for security tool processes being stopped or tampered with, changes to security-related registry keys, and gaps in expected telemetry data.

techniques.name: Process Injection
techniques.description: Adversaries may inject code into processes to evade process-based defenses and potentially elevate privileges. Running code in the context of legitimate processes helps blend into normal activity.
techniques.detection: Monitor for cross-process memory writes, remote thread creation, suspicious API call sequences (VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread), and process hollowing indicators.

techniques.name: Indicator Removal
techniques.description: Adversaries may delete or modify artifacts generated on a host or network to remove evidence of their presence. This includes clearing event logs, deleting files, and timestomping.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Deletion
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for event log clearing (Event ID 1102/104), bulk file deletions, timestamp modifications on critical files, and firewall rule deletions.

techniques.name: Masquerading
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign. This includes renaming utilities, matching legitimate file names, and invalid code signatures.
techniques.detection: Monitor for executables with names matching system binaries but running from non-standard locations, mismatched PE metadata, and invalid or unusual code signatures.

techniques.name: System Binary Proxy Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may bypass process and signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed, trusted binaries such as mshta, rundll32, regsvr32, and msiexec.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for known LOLBAS (Living Off the Land Binaries and Scripts) executing with unusual arguments, loading unexpected DLLs, or making network connections.

techniques.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may use obfuscated files or information to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis, then deobfuscate or decode them for use.
techniques.detection: Monitor for execution of decoding utilities (certutil -decode, base64), PowerShell decoding operations, and file write operations immediately following decode operations.

techniques.name: Modify Registry
techniques.description: Adversaries may interact with the Windows Registry to hide configuration information, remove information, or store data for payloads and persistence.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Creation
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Deletion
Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for changes to registry keys associated with security settings, persistence mechanisms, and known malware storage locations (e.g., registry run keys, services).

techniques.name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
techniques.description: Adversaries may employ techniques to detect and avoid virtualization and sandbox environments. Checks include looking for VM artifacts, time-based delays, and user activity.
techniques.detection: Monitor for processes checking for VM-specific registry keys, hardware identifiers, known sandbox artifacts, or implementing sleep/delay mechanisms before execution.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts
techniques.description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to blend into normal user activity, making detection difficult since the credentials appear legitimate.
techniques.detection: Monitor for behavioral anomalies in legitimate account usage, including unusual access times, geographic anomalies, and access to atypical resources.

techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping
techniques.description: Adversaries may dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material from the operating system. Targets include LSASS memory, SAM database, NTDS.dit, and DCSync.
data_sources: Active Directory: Active Directory Object Access
techniques.detection: Monitor for access to LSASS process memory, unusual access to SAM or NTDS.dit files, and execution of known credential dumping tools like Mimikatz or secretsdump.

techniques.name: Brute Force
techniques.description: Adversaries may use brute force techniques to gain access to accounts. This includes password guessing, credential stuffing, password spraying, and cracking.
techniques.detection: Monitor for multiple failed authentication attempts from single sources, distributed login failures across many accounts, and authentication anomalies indicating password spray.

techniques.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to subvert Kerberos authentication by stealing or forging Kerberos tickets. This includes Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket, Kerberoasting, and AS-REP Roasting.
data_sources: Active Directory: Active Directory Credential Request
techniques.detection: Monitor for anomalous Kerberos ticket requests (TGS requests for service accounts, encryption downgrade attacks), unusual TGT lifetimes, and tickets with forged PAC data.

techniques.name: Credentials from Password Stores
techniques.description: Adversaries may search for common password storage locations to obtain user credentials. This includes browser credential stores, Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, and password managers.
techniques.detection: Monitor for access to browser credential databases (Login Data, logins.json), Keychain queries, and credential manager enumeration via cmdkey or vaultcmd.

techniques.name: Input Capture
techniques.description: Adversaries may use methods of capturing user input to obtain credentials or collect information. This includes keylogging, GUI input capture, web portal capture, and credential API hooking.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for known keylogger signatures, API hooking of credential input functions, unexpected keyboard driver installations, and processes intercepting authentication prompts.

techniques.name: Unsecured Credentials
techniques.description: Adversaries may search compromised systems for insecurely stored credentials in files, registries, Group Policy Preferences, cloud instance metadata, and chat logs.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Access
techniques.detection: Monitor for file access to known credential storage locations, queries to cloud metadata services (169.254.169.254), and searches for files containing passwords or keys.

techniques.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to position themselves between two or more networked devices to support techniques such as credential harvesting via LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning or ARP spoofing.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning attempts, ARP spoofing, unusual DHCP responses, and network traffic interception via proxy or relay tools like Responder or Impacket.

techniques.name: Forced Authentication
techniques.description: Adversaries may gather credential material by invoking or forcing a user to automatically provide authentication information through a mechanism such as SMB relay or .lnk/.scf files.
techniques.detection: Monitor for creation of .scf or .lnk files with UNC paths, outbound SMB connections to unexpected destinations, and NTLM authentication attempts to external systems.

techniques.name: System Information Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, architecture, and connected peripherals.
techniques.detection: Monitor for execution of system information gathering commands such as systeminfo, uname, hostname, and ver in sequences that suggest enumeration.

techniques.name: Remote System Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to discover other systems on the network by querying network resources such as ARP, DNS, LDAP, and NetBIOS.
techniques.detection: Monitor for network scanning tools, ARP broadcasts, LDAP queries for computer objects, and net view or similar network enumeration commands.

techniques.name: File and Directory Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories or search in specific locations of a host or network share for certain information within a file system.
techniques.detection: Monitor for excessive directory listing commands (dir, ls, find), recursive file searches for sensitive file types, and enumeration of network shares.

techniques.name: Network Service Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts and local network infrastructure devices, including those that may be vulnerable to remote exploitation.
techniques.detection: Monitor for network port scanning activity, execution of service enumeration tools (nmap, masscan), and systematic connection attempts to multiple ports on network hosts.

techniques.name: Account Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of valid accounts, usernames, or email addresses on a system or domain, including local, domain, email, and cloud accounts.
techniques.detection: Monitor for commands that enumerate users (net user, whoami, id, Get-ADUser), LDAP queries for user objects, and cloud API calls listing accounts.

techniques.name: Permission Groups Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to discover group and permission settings on local systems, domains, or cloud environments to identify high-value targets.
techniques.detection: Monitor for group enumeration commands (net localgroup, net group, Get-ADGroup), LDAP queries for group objects, and cloud API calls listing group memberships.

techniques.name: System Network Connections Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of network connections to or from the compromised system to identify targets for lateral movement or data exfiltration.
techniques.detection: Monitor for execution of netstat, ss, or similar commands, especially in rapid succession or combined with other discovery techniques.

techniques.name: System Network Configuration Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may look for details about the network configuration and settings of systems, including IP addresses, DNS settings, gateways, and routing information.
techniques.detection: Monitor for execution of network configuration commands (ipconfig, ifconfig, route print, arp -a) and script-based network enumeration.

techniques.name: Remote Services
techniques.description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to log into a remote service to move laterally. Common services include RDP, SMB/Windows Admin Shares, SSH, VNC, and WinRM.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unexpected remote logon sessions, lateral RDP or SMB connections, unusual WinRM activity, and authentication events from non-standard source systems.

techniques.name: Lateral Tool Transfer
techniques.description: Adversaries may transfer tools or files between systems in a compromised environment using SMB shares, admin shares, or other internal file transfer methods.
techniques.detection: Monitor for file transfers between internal systems via SMB, unexpected writes to admin shares (C$, ADMIN$), and staging of tools in common directories.

techniques.name: Remote Service Session Hijacking
techniques.description: Adversaries may take control of preexisting sessions with remote services to move laterally. This includes SSH hijacking and RDP session hijacking via tscon.
techniques.detection: Monitor for tscon.exe execution for RDP session hijacking, SSH agent forwarding abuse, and unexpected session reconnections from different source IPs.

techniques.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
techniques.description: Adversaries may use alternate authentication material such as password hashes, Kerberos tickets, or application access tokens to move laterally without needing plaintext credentials.
data_sources: Active Directory: Active Directory Credential Request
techniques.detection: Monitor for pass-the-hash (NTLM authentication from unusual sources), pass-the-ticket (Kerberos ticket reuse), and authentication via stolen tokens or cookies.

techniques.name: Internal Spearphishing
techniques.description: Adversaries may use internal spearphishing to gain access to additional systems after already having a foothold, sending phishing messages from compromised internal accounts.
techniques.detection: Monitor for internal email with phishing indicators sent from recently compromised accounts, unusual email volume, and emails containing suspicious attachments or links.

techniques.name: Taint Shared Content
techniques.description: Adversaries may deliver payloads to remote systems by adding content to shared storage locations such as network drives or internal wiki/SharePoint sites.
techniques.detection: Monitor for modifications to files on network shares, especially executable or script content placed in commonly accessed locations, and unexpected file modifications on shared drives.

techniques.name: Exploitation of Remote Services
techniques.description: Adversaries may exploit remote services to gain unauthorized access to internal systems. Common targets include unpatched SMB, RDP, Apache Struts, and similar network services.
techniques.detection: Monitor for exploit signatures in network traffic, unusual service crashes or restarts, and unexpected code execution following network connections to vulnerable services.

techniques.name: Archive Collected Data
techniques.description: Adversaries may compress and/or encrypt collected data prior to exfiltration using utilities like 7zip, WinRAR, tar, or custom tools to reduce data size and evade detection.
techniques.detection: Monitor for execution of archive utilities creating compressed files in staging directories, especially with encryption flags or password-protected archives.

techniques.name: Data from Local System
techniques.description: Adversaries may search local system sources such as file systems and configuration databases for sensitive data prior to exfiltration.
techniques.detection: Monitor for processes accessing large numbers of files, unusual directory traversal patterns, and bulk file reads from sensitive directories.

techniques.name: Data from Network Shared Drive
techniques.description: Adversaries may search network shares on computers they have compromised to find files of interest for collection and exfiltration.
techniques.detection: Monitor for bulk file access across network shares, enumeration of multiple share paths, and large data transfers from shared network drives.

techniques.name: Email Collection
techniques.description: Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information. This includes accessing local email files, remote email servers, and email forwarding rules.
techniques.detection: Monitor for access to email data files (OST/PST), unusual Exchange/IMAP connections, creation of email forwarding rules, and bulk email export operations.

techniques.name: Input Capture
techniques.description: Adversaries may use methods of capturing user input to collect data typed by users, including keylogging and credential prompts.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for known keylogger patterns, API hooking of keyboard input functions, and processes intercepting or recording user input beyond normal application behavior.

techniques.name: Application Layer Protocol
techniques.description: Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection. Common protocols abused include HTTP/S, DNS, SMTP, and other web protocols.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unusual DNS query volumes or patterns, beaconing HTTP traffic, abnormal protocol usage, and domain generation algorithm indicators.

techniques.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
techniques.description: Adversaries may transfer tools or files from an external system into the compromised environment using command and control channels or alternate protocols.
techniques.detection: Monitor for new files written to disk from network connections, especially executables downloaded via PowerShell, certutil, curl, or wget.

techniques.name: Protocol Tunneling
techniques.description: Adversaries may tunnel network communications to and from a victim system within a separate protocol to avoid detection and blend in with existing traffic.
techniques.detection: Monitor for DNS tunneling indicators (high-entropy subdomains, excessive TXT records), SSH tunnels to unexpected destinations, and encapsulated traffic within allowed protocols.

techniques.name: Proxy
techniques.description: Adversaries may use a connection proxy to direct network traffic between systems or act as an intermediary. This includes internal proxies, external proxies, multi-hop proxies, and domain fronting.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unexpected proxy connections, domain fronting indicators (TLS SNI mismatch with HTTP Host header), multi-hop traffic patterns, and internal systems acting as relay nodes.

techniques.name: Encrypted Channel
techniques.description: Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic. This includes symmetric and asymmetric cryptography applied to C2 channels.
techniques.detection: Monitor for encrypted traffic to unusual destinations, use of non-standard ports with TLS/SSL, and network flows with characteristics of custom encryption.

techniques.name: Dynamic Resolution
techniques.description: Adversaries may dynamically establish connections to C2 infrastructure using domain generation algorithms, DNS calculation, or fast flux DNS to make detection harder.
techniques.detection: Monitor for DGA-like DNS queries (algorithmically generated domain names), rapidly changing DNS resolutions (fast flux), and connections to newly registered domains.

techniques.name: Non-Standard Port
techniques.description: Adversaries may communicate using a protocol and port pairing that are typically not associated to avoid detection. For example, HTTPS traffic on non-443 ports.
techniques.detection: Monitor for protocol-port mismatches (HTTP on port 8443, DNS on port 5353), unusual high-port connections, and traffic on ports not aligned with baseline network profiles.

techniques.name: Web Service
techniques.description: Adversaries may use legitimate external web services as a means for relaying C2 communications, including dead drop resolvers, bidirectional communication, and one-way communication via social media, cloud storage, or paste sites.
techniques.detection: Monitor for C2 traffic masquerading as legitimate web service usage, unusual API calls to cloud storage or social media platforms, and consistent beaconing to paste/code-sharing sites.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
techniques.description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel rather than establishing separate exfiltration channels.
techniques.detection: Monitor for large outbound data transfers over known C2 channels, unusual upload volumes, and data being staged before transmission.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
techniques.description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over a different protocol than the existing C2 channel, such as DNS, FTP, SMTP, or cloud storage APIs.
techniques.detection: Monitor for unusual outbound data transfers over protocols like DNS (large TXT queries), FTP, or SMTP from systems that do not typically use them.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
techniques.description: Adversaries may use existing legitimate external web services to exfiltrate data, including cloud storage services (Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive) and code repositories.
techniques.detection: Monitor for large uploads to cloud storage services, bulk data transfer to code repositories, and unusual file sharing activity to external accounts.

techniques.name: Automated Exfiltration
techniques.description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data automatically at scheduled intervals or in response to triggers rather than requiring manual interaction with the compromised system.
techniques.detection: Monitor for periodic outbound data transfers on fixed intervals, automated script execution collecting and transmitting data, and scheduled tasks performing data uploads.

techniques.name: Scheduled Transfer
techniques.description: Adversaries may schedule data exfiltration to be performed only at certain times of day or at certain intervals to blend with normal network activity patterns.
techniques.detection: Monitor for outbound transfers that occur at consistent times, data staging followed by timed uploads, and correlation of exfiltration timing with business hour patterns.

techniques.name: Transfer Data to Cloud Account
techniques.description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data by transferring it to a cloud account they control, leveraging cloud-to-cloud transfers that may not traverse traditional network monitoring boundaries.
techniques.detection: Monitor for cloud storage cross-account transfers, snapshot sharing with external accounts, and unusual IAM activity granting access to storage resources.

techniques.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
techniques.description: Adversaries may encrypt data on target systems to interrupt availability. Ransomware is the most common manifestation, encrypting files and demanding payment for decryption keys.
techniques.detection: Monitor for mass file modification events, unusual file extension changes, creation of ransom notes, and high entropy file writes across multiple directories.

techniques.name: Service Stop
techniques.description: Adversaries may stop or disable services on a system to render those services unavailable. Stopping critical services can inhibit incident response and recovery.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for commands that stop services (net stop, sc stop, systemctl stop), especially targeting security tools, backup services, or databases.

techniques.name: Data Destruction
techniques.description: Adversaries may destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers to interrupt availability. This may include overwriting, wiping, or deleting data from storage.
techniques.detection: Monitor for mass file deletion operations, disk wiping utilities, overwriting of MBR/VBR/partition tables, and bulk destructive operations across file systems.

techniques.name: Inhibit System Recovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may delete or remove built-in data recovery options, such as shadow copies, backup catalogs, and system restore points to prevent system recovery.
data_sources: Windows Registry: Windows Registry Key Modification
techniques.detection: Monitor for vssadmin/wmic commands deleting shadow copies, bcdedit disabling recovery, and deletion of Windows backup catalogs or Linux recovery partitions.

techniques.name: Network Denial of Service
techniques.description: Adversaries may perform Network Denial of Service (DoS) attacks to degrade or block the availability of targeted resources. This includes volumetric floods and protocol-based attacks.
techniques.detection: Monitor for abnormal spikes in network traffic volume, SYN flood indicators, unusual protocol traffic patterns, and degradation of network service availability.
